ppt.title: Chemical Reactions and Equations - Session 1
ppt.presentationGoal: To introduce the concept of chemical reactions, distinguish them from physical changes, and teach the basics of writing balanced chemical equations.

licensingNotes: Use Wikimedia Commons images by default and use in-app SVG/native slide text only as a fallback.
assetSearchPlan.fallbackStrategy: Use SVG diagrams with clear labels if specific Wikimedia assets are unavailable.

presentationWarnings: Do not confuse physical changes (melting ice) with chemical reactions.
Emphasize the Law of Conservation of Mass when balancing equations.

learningOutcomesCovered: Define a chemical reaction and identify signs

slides.teachingBeat: Set the stage for the chapter, establish relevance to daily life.
mustTeach: What is a Chemical Reaction?
Why do we need Equations?
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Chemical Reactions and Equations
> Chapter: Chemical Reactions and Equations
- What is a Chemical Reaction?
- Why do we need Equations?

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: title-slide -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Set the stage for the chapter, establish relevance to daily life.
Purpose: Introduce the chapter topic.
Explain: Start by asking students what happens when they burn wood or rust iron. Explain that these are chemical reactions, a fundamental part of chemistry.
Ask: 'Can anyone name something that changes permanently in your kitchen?'
Listen for: Cooking food, burning candles, souring milk.
Transition: Today we will learn how to describe these changes scientifically using equations.
Explain: What is a Chemical Reaction?
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Set the stage for the chapter, establish relevance to daily life.
slides.studentTakeaway: Chemical reactions are happening all around us and follow specific rules.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Set the stage for the chapter, establish relevance to daily life.
Purpose: Introduce the chapter topic.
Explain: Start by asking students what happens when they burn wood or rust iron. Explain that these are chemical reactions, a fundamental part of chemistry.
Ask: 'Can anyone name something that changes permanently in your kitchen?'
Listen for: Cooking food, burning candles, souring milk.
Transition: Today we will learn how to describe these changes scientifically using equations.
Explain: What is a Chemical Reaction?
slides.visualPlan: High-contrast image of an uncooked egg next to a frying pan with a cooking egg, illustrating transformation.

slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Today's Learning Goals
> Identify signs of a chemical reaction.
- Write word and skeletal equations for reactions.
- Balance simple chemical equations using the Law of Conservation of Mass.

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: learning-outcomes -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Clarify what students will achieve by the end of the session.
Purpose: Align student expectations with lesson goals.
Explain: Read through each point. Emphasize that 'balancing' is a key skill we will master today.
Ask: 'Why do you think it's important to balance equations?'
Listen for: To show the correct amount of matter, because atoms aren't lost.
Correct: If they say 'to make numbers even', clarify that it's about atom conservation.
Explain: Write word and skeletal equations for reactions.
-->

slides.teachingBeat: Define the core concept and distinguish it from physical changes.
mustTeach: Process where reactants transform into products.
New substance formed with different properties.
Bonds break and form; atoms rearrange.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Defining Chemical Reactions
> Process where reactants transform into products.
> New substance formed with different properties.
- Bonds break and form; atoms rearrange.

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: concept-intro -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Define the core concept and distinguish it from physical changes.
Purpose: Define the term 'Chemical Reaction'.
Explain: A chemical reaction is a process where one or more substances (reactants) change into new substances (products). The key is that the identity of the substance changes.
Ask: 'Is melting ice a chemical reaction?'
Listen for: No, it's still water.
Correct: Exactly. Melting is physical because H2O remains H2O. In a chemical reaction, like burning wood, you get ash and smoke—completely new things.
Explain: Bonds break and form; atoms rearrange.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Define the core concept and distinguish it from physical changes.
slides.studentTakeaway: A chemical reaction creates new substances with different properties.
bulletPoints: Bonds break and form; atoms rearrange.
onSlideText: Process where reactants transform into products.
New substance formed with different properties.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Define the core concept and distinguish it from physical changes.
Purpose: Define the term 'Chemical Reaction'.
Explain: A chemical reaction is a process where one or more substances (reactants) change into new substances (products). The key is that the identity of the substance changes.
Ask: 'Is melting ice a chemical reaction?'
Listen for: No, it's still water.
Correct: Exactly. Melting is physical because H2O remains H2O. In a chemical reaction, like burning wood, you get ash and smoke—completely new things.
Explain: Bonds break and form; atoms rearrange.

slides.teachingBeat: List observable evidence that a chemical change has occurred.
mustTeach: Change in Color (e.g., rusting iron)
Evolution of Gas (bubbles/fizzing)
Formation of a Precipitate (solid from liquids)
Temperature Change (heat released or absorbed)
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# How Do We Know It Happened?
> Change in Color (e.g., rusting iron)
- Evolution of Gas (bubbles/fizzing)
- Formation of a Precipitate (solid from liquids)
- Temperature Change (heat released or absorbed)

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: evidence-slide -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: List observable evidence that a chemical change has occurred.
Purpose: Provide observable evidence for students to identify reactions.
Explain: We can't always see atoms moving, but we see the effects. Color change is common in rusting. Gas evolution happens when acid hits metal. Precipitate forms when two clear liquids mix and turn cloudy. Temperatu...
Ask: 'What do you feel if a reaction releases heat?'
Listen for: It gets hot.
Board work: Draw a simple diagram of bubbles rising from a beaker to illustrate gas evolution.
Explain: Evolution of Gas (bubbles/fizzing)
-->
slides.slideTitle: How Do We Know It Happened?
slides.teacherIntent: List observable evidence that a chemical change has occurred.
slides.studentTakeaway: We can spot reactions by color changes, gas production, etc.
bulletPoints: Formation of a Precipitate (solid from liquids)
speakerNotes: Purpose: List observable evidence that a chemical change has occurred.
Purpose: Provide observable evidence for students to identify reactions.

Explain: We can't always see atoms moving, but we see the effects. Color change is common in rusting. Gas evolution happens when acid hits metal. Precipitate forms when two clear liquids mix and turn cloudy. Temperatu...
Ask: 'What do you feel if a reaction releases heat?'
Listen for: It gets hot.
Board work: Draw a simple diagram of bubbles rising from a beaker to illustrate gas evolution.
Explain: Evolution of Gas (bubbles/fizzing)

slides.teachingBeat: Demonstrate how to convert a word description into a skeletal chemical equation.
mustTeach: Word Equation: Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium Oxide
Skeletal Equation: Mg(s) + O₂(g) → MgO(s)
Reactants (Left): Substances starting the reaction.
Products (Right): New substances formed.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# From Words to Symbols: Magnesium Burning
> Word Equation: Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium Oxide
> Skeletal Equation: Mg(s) + O₂(g) → MgO(s)
- Reactants (Left): Substances starting the reaction.
- Products (Right): New substances formed.
![From Words to Symbols: Magnesium Burning](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/9bf1d56dba1211f3f71d411a670b0eff35875d54.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: worked-example-1 -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Demonstrate how to convert a word description into a skeletal chemical equation.
Purpose: Teach the structure of a chemical equation.
Explain: Start with words. Then replace names with symbols and formulas. Remember, Oxygen is diatomic (O₂), not just O. The arrow means 'yields' or 'produces'.
Ask: 'Which side are the reactants on?'
Listen for: Left.
Board work: Write Mg + O2 -> MgO clearly on the board, highlighting the subscripts.
Explain: Reactants (Left): Substances starting the reaction.
-->
slides.slideTitle: From Words to Symbols: Magnesium Burning
slides.teacherIntent: Demonstrate how to convert a word description into a skeletal chemical equation.
slides.studentTakeaway: Reactants go on the left, products on the right, separated by an arrow.
onSlideText: Word Equation: Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium Oxide
speakerNotes: Purpose: Demonstrate how to convert a word description into a skeletal chemical equation.
Purpose: Teach the structure of a chemical equation.
Explain: Start with words. Then replace names with symbols and formulas. Remember, Oxygen is diatomic (O₂), not just O. The arrow means 'yields' or 'produces'.
Ask: 'Which side are the reactants on?'
Listen for: Left.
Board work: Write Mg + O2 -> MgO clearly on the board, highlighting the subscripts.
Explain: Reactants (Left): Substances starting the reaction.

slides.teachingBeat: Introduce the Law of Conservation of Mass and demonstrate balancing.
mustTeach: Law: Matter is conserved (atoms count stays same).
Unbalanced: Mg + O₂ → MgO (2 Oxygen on left, 1 on right)
Balanced: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO (Equal atoms both sides)
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Why We Must Balance Equations
> Law: Matter is conserved (atoms count stays same).
- Unbalanced: Mg + O₂ → MgO (2 Oxygen on left, 1 on right)
- Balanced: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO (Equal atoms both sides)

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: worked-example-2 -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Introduce the Law of Conservation of Mass and demonstrate balancing.
Purpose: Explain the necessity of balancing equations.
Explain: The unbalanced equation shows we have two oxygen atoms on the left but only one on the right. This violates the Law of Conservation of Mass. We fix this by adding coefficients (numbers in front).
Ask: 'What happens if we put a 2 in front of MgO?'
Listen for: Now there are 2 oxygens.
Correct: Yes, but now Magnesium is unbalanced again! So we need a 2 in front of Mg too.
Explain: Unbalanced: Mg + O₂ → MgO (2 Oxygen on left, 1 on right)
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Introduce the Law of Conservation of Mass and demonstrate balancing.
slides.studentTakeaway: Atoms are neither created nor destroyed; we must balance them with coefficients.
bulletPoints: Unbalanced: Mg + O₂ → MgO (2 Oxygen on left, 1 on right)
onSlideText: Law: Matter is conserved (atoms count stays same).
speakerNotes: Purpose: Introduce the Law of Conservation of Mass and demonstrate balancing.
Purpose: Explain the necessity of balancing equations.

Explain: The unbalanced equation shows we have two oxygen atoms on the left but only one on the right. This violates the Law of Conservation of Mass. We fix this by adding coefficients (numbers in front).
Ask: 'What happens if we put a 2 in front of MgO?'
Listen for: Now there are 2 oxygens.
Correct: Yes, but now Magnesium is unbalanced again! So we need a 2 in front of Mg too.
Explain: Unbalanced: Mg + O₂ → MgO (2 Oxygen on left, 1 on right)
slides.wikimediaSearchLabel: we must balance equations balancing equations
slides.visualPlan: A labelled classroom diagram for Why We Must Balance Equations that makes the process or structure visible while the teacher explains worked-example-2.

slides.teachingBeat: Guide students through a second balancing problem to reinforce the skill.
mustTeach: Step 1: Count atoms on both sides.
Step 2: Add coefficient to balance Oxygen (needs 2 H₂O).
Step 3: Balance Hydrogen (now needs 2 H₂).
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Practice Balancing: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O
> Step 1: Count atoms on both sides.
- Step 2: Add coefficient to balance Oxygen (needs 2 H₂O).
- Step 3: Balance Hydrogen (now needs 2 H₂).

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: guided-practice -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Guide students through a second balancing problem to reinforce the skill.
Purpose: Guided practice for balancing.
Explain: Let's look at hydrogen burning. Left side has 2 O, right has 1. We need to double the water (H₂O). Now we have 4 H on the right, so we must put a 2 in front of H₂ on the left.
Ask: 'What is the final balanced equation?'
Listen for: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O.
Board work: Write the steps clearly as students suggest them.
Explain: Step 2: Add coefficient to balance Oxygen (needs 2 H₂O).
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Guide students through a second balancing problem to reinforce the skill.
slides.studentTakeaway: I can apply the steps of counting atoms and adding coefficients myself.
bulletPoints: Step 2: Add coefficient to balance Oxygen (needs 2 H₂O).
onSlideText: Step 1: Count atoms on both sides.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Guide students through a second balancing problem to reinforce the skill.
Purpose: Guided practice for balancing.
Explain: Let's look at hydrogen burning. Left side has 2 O, right has 1. We need to double the water (H₂O). Now we have 4 H on the right, so we must put a 2 in front of H₂ on the left.
Ask: 'What is the final balanced equation?'
Listen for: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O.
Board work: Write the steps clearly as students suggest them.
Explain: Step 2: Add coefficient to balance Oxygen (needs 2 H₂O).
slides.wikimediaSearchLabel: practice balancing h₂ o₂ h₂o balancing
slides.visualPlan: A labelled classroom diagram for Practice Balancing: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O that makes the process or structure visible while the teacher explains guided-practice.

slides.teachingBeat: Consolidate learning and ensure retention of key points.
mustTeach: Reactions form new substances with different properties.
Equations use symbols: Reactants → Products.
Balancing ensures atom counts match (Conservation of Mass).
Coefficients adjust quantities, not subscripts.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Key Takeaways from Today
> Reactions form new substances with different properties.
- Equations use symbols: Reactants → Products.
- Balancing ensures atom counts match (Conservation of Mass).
- Coefficients adjust quantities, not subscripts.

<!-- layout: text-light -->
<!-- purpose: summary-recap -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Consolidate learning and ensure retention of key points.
Purpose: Summarize the session's core learning points.
Explain: Review each point. Emphasize that we never change subscripts (like changing H2O to HO) because that changes the substance itself; we only use coefficients.
Explain: Equations use symbols: Reactants → Products.
Explain: Balancing ensures atom counts match (Conservation of Mass).
Explain: Coefficients adjust quantities, not subscripts.
Explain: Student takeaway: Chemical reactions create new substances, are written as equations, and must be balanced.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Consolidate learning and ensure retention of key points.
slides.studentTakeaway: Chemical reactions create new substances, are written as equations, and must be balanced.
bulletPoints: Balancing ensures atom counts match (Conservation of Mass).
onSlideText: Reactions form new substances with different properties.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Consolidate learning and ensure retention of key points.
Purpose: Summarize the session's core learning points.
Explain: Review each point. Emphasize that we never change subscripts (like changing H2O to HO) because that changes the substance itself; we only use coefficients.
Explain: Equations use symbols: Reactants → Products.
Explain: Balancing ensures atom counts match (Conservation of Mass).
Explain: Coefficients adjust quantities, not subscripts.
Explain: Student takeaway: Chemical reactions create new substances, are written as equations, and must be balanced.
